content.text: Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG) is Azure's default pricing model: you pay only for the resources you consume, billed monthly with no upfront commitment. Usage is measured by meters — counters that track consumption in specific units (hours, GB, requests, vCore-hours) and multiply the consumed quantity by the per-unit rate for the billing period.

PAYG is the most flexible Azure pricing tier. It carries the highest per-unit rates compared to Reserved Instances or Savings Plans, but requires no upfront commitment and can scale to zero cost when resources are deallocated or deleted.

attrs.alt: Pay-As-You-Go meter anatomy and common pricing dimensions

content.text: Each Azure resource type is measured by one or more meters. A meter runs from the moment the resource is provisioned and stops when the resource is deallocated (for VMs) or deleted. At the end of the billing period, Azure multiplies the total units consumed by the meter rate to produce the line-item cost.
Billed per second; stops on deallocation, not shutdown
Billed even when VM is deallocated
Dynamic IPs free when unassociated; static always billed
Azure Pricing Calculator is the authoritative source for PAYG rates. Rates vary by region, service tier, and currency. The CLI can query cost data from the Cost Management API for actual spend.

# View retail prices for a specific service and region via the Retail Prices API
az rest --method get \
  --url "https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices?api-version=2023-01-01-preview" \
  --url-parameters "\\$filter=serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' and armRegionName eq 'eastus' and skuName eq 'D2s v3'" \
  --query 'Items[:3].{sku:skuName,price:retailPrice,unit:unitOfMeasure}' \
  -o table

import requests

# Query Azure Retail Prices API (no authentication needed)
url = 'https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices'
params = {
    'api-version': '2023-01-01-preview',
    '$filter': (
        "serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' "
        "and armRegionName eq 'eastus' "
        "and skuName eq 'D2s v3' "
        "and priceType eq 'Consumption'"
    )
}
resp = requests.get(url, params=params)
items = resp.json().get('Items', [])

for item in items:
    print(
        f"{item['skuName']:<30} "
        f"{item['retailPrice']:>8.4f} "
        f"{item['currencyCode']} / {item['unitOfMeasure']}"
    )

A common mistake is stopping a VM via the OS (guest shutdown) rather than through Azure. A guest OS shutdown sets the VM's power state to Stopped but keeps it allocated — Azure continues billing compute hours. Only deallocating the VM stops compute billing.

# Guest OS shutdown (still billed — DO NOT rely on this to stop charges)
# ssh user@vm 'sudo shutdown now'  <- this does NOT stop billing

# Azure deallocation — stops compute billing
az vm deallocate \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM

# Verify the VM is deallocated (not just stopped)
az vm show \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --query 'powerState' \
  -d -o tsv
# Expected: VM deallocated

# Restart from deallocated state
az vm start \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM

Managed disks, static public IPs, and reserved network interfaces continue to accrue charges even when the VM is deallocated. To stop all charges, delete the VM and its associated resources — or use auto-shutdown schedules for dev/test VMs.

# Enable auto-shutdown at 7PM EST every day
az vm auto-shutdown \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --time 1900 \
  --email devteam@company.com

# Disable auto-shutdown
az vm auto-shutdown \
  --resource-group myRG \
  --name myVM \
  --off
Querying Actual Spend via Cost Management

# View month-to-date cost for a resource group
az costmanagement query \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)/resourceGroups/myRG" \
  --type ActualCost \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"name":"ResourceType","type":"Dimension"}]' \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --query 'properties.rows' \
  -o table

# Top 10 most expensive resources in the subscription
az costmanagement query \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --type ActualCost \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"name":"ResourceId","type":"Dimension"}]' \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --dataset-filter '{"and":[{"dimensions":{"name":"ResourceId","operator":"In","values":["*"]}}]}' \
  -o json 2>/dev/null | python3 -c "
import json, sys
data = json.load(sys.stdin)
rows = data.get('properties', {}).get('rows', [])
sorted_rows = sorted(rows, key=lambda r: float(r[0]), reverse=True)[:10]
for row in sorted_rows:
    print(f'  {float(row[0]):>10.2f} USD  {row[1]}')
"
Orgs with active SA on Windows Server or SQL Server
None — can be evicted with 30s notice

Start with PAYG to measure actual usage patterns over 2-4 weeks, then purchase Reserved Instances for the steady-state baseline. Savings Plans layer on top and cover the variable portion. This hybrid approach typically achieves 40-55% total savings over pure PAYG.
Azure Cost Management lets you set spending budgets with alert thresholds so you are notified before costs exceed expectations.

# Create a monthly budget with email alert at 80% and 100% of threshold
az consumption budget create \
  --budget-name MyMontlyBudget \
  --amount 500 \
  --category Cost \
  --time-grain Monthly \
  --start-date 2024-03-01 \
  --end-date 2025-03-01 \
  --notifications '[{
    "enabled": true,
    "operator": "GreaterThan",
    "threshold": 80,
    "contactEmails": ["finops@company.com"],
    "thresholdType": "Actual"
  }, {
    "enabled": true,
    "operator": "GreaterThan",
    "threshold": 100,
    "contactEmails": ["cto@company.com"],
    "thresholdType": "Forecasted"
  }]'

# List all budgets
az consumption budget list -o table
The Azure Cost Management SDK gives programmatic access to usage and cost data, enabling custom reporting dashboards and automated cost allocation workflows.

from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.mgmt.costmanagement import CostManagementClient
from azure.mgmt.costmanagement.models import (
    QueryDefinition, QueryTimePeriod, QueryDataset,
    QueryAggregation, QueryGrouping, TimeframeType
)
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
client = CostManagementClient(credential)

scope = f'/subscriptions/{subscription_id}'

# Query last 30 days of actual cost grouped by service name
end = datetime.utcnow()
start = end - timedelta(days=30)

result = client.query.usage(
    scope=scope,
    parameters=QueryDefinition(
        type='ActualCost',
        timeframe=TimeframeType.CUSTOM,
        time_period=QueryTimePeriod(
            from_property=start,
            to=end,
        ),
        dataset=QueryDataset(
            granularity='None',
            aggregation={
                'totalCost': QueryAggregation(
                    name='Cost', function='Sum'
                )
            },
            grouping=[
                QueryGrouping(type='Dimension', name='ServiceName')
            ],
        ),
    ),
)

# Print cost per service, sorted descending
rows = result.rows
sorted_rows = sorted(rows, key=lambda r: float(r[0]), reverse=True)
print(f'{'Service':<40} {'Cost (USD)':>12}')
print('-' * 54)
for row in sorted_rows:
    print(f'{str(row[1]):<40} {float(row[0]):>12.2f}')

Inbound data transfer to Azure is always free. Outbound data transfer (egress) is charged per GB after the first 100 GB per month. The egress rate depends on the destination zone and applies to data leaving an Azure datacenter — including API responses, blob downloads, and VM traffic destined for the internet or other regions.
Azure region A → Azure region B
Cross-region egress; rates vary by zone pairing
Billed on both sides of the transfer

Place services that talk to each other in the same Azure region to avoid egress charges. For multi-region architectures, use Azure CDN to serve static assets closer to users and reduce origin egress costs.
Free Services and Free Tier Limits
Many Azure services include a monthly free grant that resets on the first of each month. These are not trial-only — they apply permanently to any subscription on the PAYG or EA model.
10 web apps on F1 tier (shared compute)
Varies by service (e.g. 5k text translation chars/month)

# Check which free services are available in your subscription
az resource list \
  --query '[?sku.tier==`Free`].{name:name,type:type,sku:sku.name}' \
  -o table

# View your current month usage for Functions (free grant tracking)
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Web/sites/<funcApp>" \
  --metric 'FunctionExecutionCount' \
  --interval P1D \
  --start-time $(date -u -d 'first day of month' '+%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z' 2>/dev/null || date -u '+%Y-%m-01T00:00:00Z') \
  --end-time $(date -u '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ') \
  --query 'value[0].timeseries[0].data[-1]' \
  -o json
PAYG costs accumulate from resources that are provisioned but underutilized. Azure Advisor identifies idle and underutilized resources and recommends right-sizing or shutdown.

# List Azure Advisor cost recommendations
az advisor recommendation list \
  --category Cost \
  --query '[].{impact:impact,type:impactedField,resource:impactedValue,savings:extendedProperties.annualSavingsAmount}' \
  -o table

# Find VMs with < 5% average CPU over the last 7 days (underutilized)
az monitor metrics list \
  --resource "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/<rg>/providers/Microsoft.Compute/virtualMachines/<vm>" \
  --metric 'Percentage CPU' \
  --interval P1D \
  --aggregation Average \
  --start-time $(date -u -d '7 days ago' '+%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z' 2>/dev/null || date -u -v -7d '+%Y-%m-%dT00:00:00Z') \
  --end-time $(date -u '+%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ') \
  --query 'value[0].timeseries[0].data[].average | avg(@)' \
  -o tsv
Idle Load Balancers: LB frontend IPs bill even with zero traffic. Delete or reassign frontends not in use.
Orphaned managed disks: Disks that are not attached to any VM still incur storage costs. Identify and delete with az disk list --query '[?diskState==`Unattached`]'.
Unused Public IPs: Static public IPs charge ~$0.005/hour when associated with a stopped/deallocated VM. Delete or set to Dynamic.
Empty App Service Plans: ASPs with zero apps still bill for the compute tier. Delete or downgrade to Free tier.
Idle Azure SQL: Single databases not queried for 7+ days can be scaled down or converted to serverless tier with auto-pause.
Exporting Usage Data for Custom Reporting
Azure allows you to export detailed usage data to a storage account on a scheduled basis (daily or monthly). This enables integration with BI tools, cost allocation models, and custom dashboards.

# Create a cost export to a storage account (daily, current month)
az costmanagement export create \
  --name DailyUsageExport \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --type ActualCost \
  --dataset-configuration '{"columns":["UsageDate","ResourceId","ResourceType","MeterName","Quantity","EffectivePrice","Cost","Tags"]}' \
  --schedule-status Active \
  --schedule-recurrence Daily \
  --recurrence-period from=2024-03-01T00:00:00Z to=2025-03-01T00:00:00Z \
  --storage-account-id "/subscriptions/<sub>/resourceGroups/billing-rg/providers/Microsoft.Storage/storageAccounts/mybillingstore" \
  --storage-container billing-exports \
  --storage-directory daily

# List existing exports
az costmanagement export list \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --query '[].{name:name,status:properties.schedule.status,lastRun:properties.lastRun}' \
  -o table

import csv, gzip
from azure.storage.blob import BlobServiceClient
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
blob_client = BlobServiceClient(
    account_url='https://mybillingstore.blob.core.windows.net',
    credential=credential
)
container = blob_client.get_container_client('billing-exports')

# Find the latest export blob
blobs = sorted(
    container.list_blobs(name_starts_with='daily/'),
    key=lambda b: b.last_modified,
    reverse=True
)
if not blobs:
    raise RuntimeError('No export blobs found')

latest = blobs[0]
data = container.download_blob(latest.name).readall()

# Parse CSV (exports are gzipped)
with gzip.open(__import__('io').BytesIO(data)) as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(__import__('io').TextIOWrapper(f))
    by_service = {}
    for row in reader:
        svc = row.get('ServiceName', 'Unknown')
        cost = float(row.get('Cost', 0))
        by_service[svc] = by_service.get(svc, 0) + cost

for svc, total in sorted(by_service.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)[:10]:
    print(f'{svc:<40} {total:>10.2f} USD')

Tags are the primary mechanism for attributing PAYG costs to teams, projects, or cost centers. Azure Cost Management can group and filter cost reports by tag values, enabling chargeback and showback reporting.

# Apply cost-center and environment tags to all resources in a resource group
az resource list --resource-group myRG --query '[].id' -o tsv | while read ID; do
  az resource tag \
    --ids "$ID" \
    --tags cost-center=platform environment=prod team=infra
done

# Query costs grouped by cost-center tag
az costmanagement query \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$(az account show --query id -o tsv)" \
  --type ActualCost \
  --dataset-aggregation '{"totalCost":{"name":"Cost","function":"Sum"}}' \
  --dataset-grouping '[{"name":"cost-center","type":"TagKey"}]' \
  --timeframe MonthToDate \
  --query 'properties.rows' \
  -o json | python3 -c "
import json,sys
rows = json.load(sys.stdin)
for r in sorted(rows, key=lambda x: float(x[0]), reverse=True):
    print(f'{str(r[1]):<30} {float(r[0]):>12.2f} USD')
"

Tags applied to a resource group do not automatically inherit to child resources in cost reports — tag each resource individually, or use Azure Policy with the 'Inherit tag from resource group' effect to enforce consistent tagging.

Before provisioning resources, use the Azure Pricing Calculator to build a monthly cost estimate. The Total Cost of Ownership (TCO) Estimator compares on-premises infrastructure costs against an equivalent Azure deployment — useful for migration business cases.

# The Pricing Calculator is web-based:
# https://azure.microsoft.com/en-us/pricing/calculator/

# You can export a pricing estimate as JSON and query it:
# 1. Build your estimate in the calculator
# 2. Click 'Export' -> JSON
# 3. Parse with Python to automate estimate comparisons

# Alternatively, query the Retail Prices API to estimate costs programmatically:
python3 << 'EOF'
import requests

def get_vm_hourly_rate(sku, region='eastus'):
    url = 'https://prices.azure.com/api/retail/prices'
    params = {
        'api-version': '2023-01-01-preview',
        '$filter': (
            f"serviceName eq 'Virtual Machines' "
            f"and armRegionName eq '{region}' "
            f"and skuName eq '{sku}' "
            f"and priceType eq 'Consumption' "
            f"and contains(productName, 'Windows') eq false"
        )
    }
    resp = requests.get(url, params=params)
    items = resp.json().get('Items', [])
    if not items:
        return None
    return items[0]['retailPrice']

skus = ['D2s v3', 'D4s v3', 'E2s v3']
for sku in skus:
    rate = get_vm_hourly_rate(sku)
    if rate:
        monthly = rate * 730  # 730 hours/month average
        print(f'{sku:<15} ${rate:.4f}/hr  ~${monthly:.2f}/month')
EOF

The Retail Prices API returns list (PAYG) prices. If your organization has an EA or MCA agreement, your actual rates may differ. Use the Azure Cost Management API with actual billing data for accurate reporting.

Pay-As-You-Go is Azure's default model: resources are metered by unit (hour, GB, request) and charged at list price with no commitment. VMs must be deallocated — not merely shut down via the guest OS — to stop compute billing. Managed disks and static IPs bill independently of VM power state. Use the Azure Retail Prices API to query current rates programmatically, Cost Management to analyze actual spend, and budgets with thresholds to alert on unexpected cost increases. PAYG is ideal for variable or short-lived workloads; for steady-state production usage, Reserved Instances or Savings Plans reduce costs by 30-65%.
